coredata.dc:title: Quantification and visualization of penicillin G residues in milk using a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on carbon dots and gold nanoclusters
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Asadpour, Saied
Saberi, Zeinab
Naderi, Marzieh
coredata.dc:description: Developing an effective assay for monitoring penicillin G (PNG) is important for safeguarding ensuring public health and food safety. This work details the application of carbon dots (CDs) emitting blue light and gold nanoclusters (GNCs) with red emission as ratiometric fluorescence (RF) probes for the sensitive detection of PNG. The RF probe under a single excitation of 360 nm wavelength displayed two discrete emission peaks at 444 and 660 nm related to CDs and GNCs, respectively. Following the introduction of PNG, the red fluorescence intensity of GNCs was found to be reduced through the inner filter effect (IFE); meanwhile, the blue fluorescence of CDs remained unchanged under optimal conditions. The quantification of PNG can be achieved by this RF nanoprobe by following the I660/I444 ratio in two ranges of 0.1–0.8 ppm and 0.8–30.0 ppm and with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 30 ppb. Furthermore, the RF probe has been successfully employed to determine PNG in milk samples with acceptable recoveries and results were validated by HPLC. Finally, the visual capability of this RF probe can greatly enhance and facilitate on-site determination process of PNG.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Penicillin G
Carbon dots
Gold nanoclusters
Ratiometric fluorescence method
Milk

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure of PNG detection by an RF probe
Design and mechanism of the RF probe

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been supported by the research deputy of Shahrekord university. The grant number was 141/1402/13.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The UV–visible absorption spectra of CDs (a) and GNCs (b); (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (a) and GNCs (b) (the insets are photographs of CDs and GNCs under UV light); (C) FT-IR spectra of CDs; and (D) GNCs.
(A) Tapping mode AFM images and (B) high profiles of CDs; (C) TEM image and (D) size distribution of GNCs.
Observed (Eobs) and corrected (Ecor) suppressed efficiency of PNG toward GNCs.
(A) Response of the RF probe at different pH in the absence and presence of 15 ppm PNG, (B) over time up to 12 min in the presence of 5 ppm PNG.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of RF sensor with increasing concentrations of PNG (0.1–30.0 ppm) under optimal conditions; (B) The linear curves of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I660/I444)0/(I660/I444) versus the concentration.

Selectivity test of the RF probe toward PNG (A) in the individual solutions with a concentration of 15 ppm, (B) in the mixture of 15 ppm PNG by the interfering species with the concentration of 15 ppm.
Schematic description of sensing platform based on CDs and GNCs for detection of PNG.
Comparison between reported methods for the determination of PNG.
Sensing parameters of PNG detection in milk samples.
Validation study for PNG detection in milk samples.

head.ce:title: Quantification and visualization of penicillin G residues in milk using a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on carbon dots and gold nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Shahrekord University, Shahrekord 115, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Shahrekord University, Shahrekord 115, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Institute, Shahrekord University, 8818634141, Shahrekord, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Institute, Shahrekord University, 8818634141, Shahrekord, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Shahrekord University, Shahrekord 115, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing an effective assay for monitoring penicillin G (PNG) is important for safeguarding ensuring public health and food safety. This work details the application of carbon dots (CDs) emitting blue light and gold nanoclusters (GNCs) with red emission as ratiometric fluorescence (RF) probes for the sensitive detection of PNG. The RF probe under a single excitation of 360 nm wavelength displayed two discrete emission peaks at 444 and 660 nm related to CDs and GNCs, respectively. Following the introduction of PNG, the red fluorescence intensity of GNCs was found to be reduced through the inner filter effect (IFE); meanwhile, the blue fluorescence of CDs remained unchanged under optimal conditions. The quantification of PNG can be achieved by this RF nanoprobe by following the I660/I444 ratio in two ranges of 0.1–0.8 ppm and 0.8–30.0 ppm and with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 30 ppb. Furthermore, the RF probe has been successfully employed to determine PNG in milk samples with acceptable recoveries and results were validated by HPLC. Finally, the visual capability of this RF probe can greatly enhance and facilitate on-site determination process of PNG.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have been indispensable in safeguarding human health since the discovery of the first antibiotic. Over the years, their production has surged significantly and served as powerful tools in the prevention and treatment of bacterial ailments among humans. Moreover, they have found utility in bolstering animal growth [1,2].

The β-lactam class of antibiotics has garnered considerable attention owing to their remarkable effectiveness and specificity and further, amplified by the diverse range of available derivatives [3]. Among the β-lactams, the penicillin group is widely applied due to its potent pharmacological effects. Research has indicted that penicillin at high dose is less toxic and more cost-effective compared to tetracycline [4]. However, indiscriminate and excessive usage and misuse of these antibiotics in animal feed results in their entry into the food chain, thereby endangering human health [5,6]. Improper use can have significant health consequences such as allergic reactions, antibiotic resistance, the emergence of bacteria resistant to multiple drugs, and death [7]. To avoid the harmful impacts of β-lactam residues, maximum residue limits (MRL) have been considered. This value for penicillin G (PNG) in milk is 1.2 × 10−8 M [8].

Currently, the standard methods used to identify β-lactams are enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) [9], gas chromatography (GC) [10], liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (LC/MS) [11], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [12]. Despite the fact that their sensitivity reaches a few nanomolar range, these approaches are a laborious and time-consuming process and need a central laboratory with expensive equipment and professional technical personnel. Accordingly, it is highly vital to develop a sensitive, affordable, rapid, and simple assay [13].

Recently, advances in nanomaterials and biological technology have led to rapid developments in the construction of different (bio)sensors for the detection of antibiotics. Sensors based on antibodies, aptamers [14], or molecularly imprinted polymer [15] are on different detection principles, such as colorimetry [16], fluorescence [17], electrochemistry [6], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [18], reflectomteric interference spectroscopy (RFIS) and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [19]. Among these approaches, fluorescence-based sensing has attracted much attention due to its high sensitivity, simple operation, rapidity and good selectivity [20]. Extensive attention to this method is due to the utilization of different fluorescent nanomaterials as fluorophores, such as carbon dots (CDs) and metal nanoclusters (MNCs) [21].

CDs as a novel group of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with particle diameter <10 nm have attracted significant attention due to their unique properties such as simple synthesis, good biocompatibility, water solubility, high chemical stability and photostability, high fluorescence and very low toxicity. These properties have made them a great promise in the optical and biological sensing [22,23].

Nobel MNCs, as a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials, have emerged as promising analytical tools in the sensing field thanks to fascinating features including optical and chemical stability, long luminescence lifetime, a large Stokes shift and good biocompatibility. The structure of MNCs can be characterized as a core-shell arrangement. A central metallic core is encompassed by an outer shell comprising organic molecular ligands [24,25]. In the last two decades, noble MNCs, particularly gold nanoclusters (GNCs) have attracted copious research attention for their unique optical properties, and ultrasmall size (<2 nm), high surface-to-volume ratio and good photoluminescence, which is one of the most attractive properties of GNCs. The synthesis of GNCs typically involves the bottom-up approach, where they are stabilized using various substances such as bovine serum albumin (BSA), lysozyme and more. Organic molecular ligands significantly contributed to the enhancement of GNCs' fluorescence [26,27].

In fluorescence techniques based on monitoring a single wavelength, a variety of analyte-independent factors might disturb the fluorescence signal. These factors include light scattering generated by the sample matrix, photobleaching of the fluorophore and excitation source fluctuations that decrease the method's reliability [28,29]. In order to tackle these issues, an RF technique can be used to determine analyte concentrations using the ratio between two or more fluorescence signals at different emission wavelengths. In addition, the determination based on ratiometry reduces an effective internal referencing that significantly improves the sensitivity and selectivity of system [30,31]. Also, one of the remarkable properties of RF method is ability to make visual determinations using a simple UV lamp by creating color variations.

Color changes in RF probes are easier to detect by the human eyes compared with changes in the color brightness of single color fluorescence probes. That's why RF probes are preferred for visual determinations [32].

In the present research, a ratiometric-based fluorescent sensor was developed by a simple combination of CDs and GNCs as two dynamic fluorophores. Considering the importance of detection of PNG, it was used as the target analyte. Scheme 1 schematically demonstrates the principle of sensing. In this system, the fluoresce of CDs (λmax = 444 nm) was quenched in percent of GNCs using the FRET mechanism. Upon addition of PNG to the system, the fluorescence intensity of the GNCs (λmax = 660 nm) was decreased based on IEF; In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained unchanged under a single excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The RF probe was able to provide a color range related to various PNG concentrations.
To our knowledge, this is the first paper regarding the RF analysis of PNG using CDs and GNCs.

All substances including gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, 99 %), bovine serum albumin (BSA), citric acid, ethylenediamine (EDA), NaOH, KNO3, NaNO3, tetracycline (TC), metronidazole (MTZ), ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (GS), ciprofloxacin (CF), Vitamin A (V-A), Vitamin E (V–B), Lysine (Lys) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich.

The microwave synthesis was performed by a CEM Discover-SP. UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a Pharmacia 4000 spectrophotometer at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were measured by a Shimadzu RF-5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer. A PerkinElmer 2000 spectrometer was used for recording FT-IR spectra. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were taken by a Nanoscope IIIa, VEECO Instrument. Transmission electron micrograph (TEM) analysis was gained by Philips, Model CM30. A Kaiser test was utilized to guess the amount of primary amino groups on the CDs surface.

The BSA-capped GNCs have been synthesized according to the previous procedure published in the literature. Typically, a mixture of HAuCl4 solution (5 mL, 10 mM) and BSA solution (5 mL, 50 mg/mL) was vigorously stirred for 10 min. Next, 0.5 mL of NaOH (1 M) solution was added to adjust pH around 11 and incubated for 12 h at room temperature. In order to remove excess chemicals from the obtained red-orange product, the solution was first filtered via a 0.22 μm membrane filter and following, dialyzed through a dialysis membrane.

CDs with amino groups were provided based on the previously reported protocol from citric acid and urea with some modification [33]. In a typical process, a mixture of citric acid and urea (1:1, 0.75 g) dissolved in the deionized water (2.5 mL) were heated at 200 °C and 15 bar for 300 s, followed by filtration (0.22 μm), dialysis against water for 48 h. The product is dark brown colored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure of PNG detection by an RF probe
ce:section.ce:para: The RF probe was prepared by a simple mixing 200 μL of GNCs and 20 μL of CDs in the phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH = 7). The fluorescence signal of CDs-GNCs was measured in the range 370–800 nm while being excited at 360 nm, which served as the control signal. Afterward, PNG with the desired concentrations was added to the probe solution. After incubating for 11 min, the ratio of fluorescence intensity of two distinctive emission wavelengths of 444 and 660 nm was collected as sample signals. The selectivity of the sensing system was investigated by the addition of various potential interferences including K+, Na+, ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (GS), metronidazole (MTZ), tetracycline (TC) and ciprofloxacin (CF) into the prepared probe solution.

To confirm the practicality of the RF probe, the sensing experiment was performed on the pasteurized and raw milk samples. The milk samples were diluted with a buffer solution (1:10, v/v) and then, centrifuged at 20000 rpm for 20 min. Milk samples were passed from a 0.22 μm filtration membrane. The different known concentrations of PNG were spiked to samples [34].

CDs were prepared from citric acid and urea as carbon and nitrogen sources by microwave-assisted technique. GNCs capped in BSA were synthesized by a self-assembly method. Optical and electronical features of CDs and GNCs were characterized by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopies. The UV–Vis absorbance spectra of CDs and GNCs are displayed in Fig. 1 A. In the UV–Vis spectrum of CDs, there is an absorption peak centered at 340 nm. Additionally, there is a smaller peak at 410 nm, accompanied by a tail that extends into the visible range. They are attributed to the π-π∗ and n-π∗ transition of the CC and CO units present in the core of the CDs. GNCs have a continuous absorption spectrum without any prominent peak. The fluorescence emission is considered one of the most outstanding characterizations of CDs and GNCs. As shown in Fig. 1B, the fluorescence spectra of CDs and GNCs present maximum emissions at 444 and 660 nm with 360 nm excitation wavelength [35,36].

The structural information of CDs and GNCs can be obtained by FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs in Fig. 1C shows that bands centered at 3300 and 1660 cm−1 are indicative of O–H/N–H stretching modes and N–H bond. While, C–H and CO bonds can be recognized with 2940 and 1708 cm−1. Furthermore, the absorptions at 1390 and 1577 cm−1 are related to C–N and CN, respectively. A value of the Kaiser test was obtained 1300 μmol g−1 that showing the presence of abundant amino groups. FT-IR spectrum of GNCs in Fig. 1D displays the peaks around 3450 and 2956 cm−1 attributed to –NH/-OH and –CH stretching vibrations, respectively. Characteristic peaks at 1660, 1550 and1290 cm−1 defined amide I, II and III bands, respectively [35,36].

The morphology and size of CDs and GNCs were explored by AFM and TEM methods, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. S1, CDs with a spherical shape have an average size of around 5.0 nm. From TEM image, it can be perceived that GNCs were monodispersed and their average size is about 4.0 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and mechanism of the RF probe
ce:section.ce:para: A dual-emission fluorescent probe was constructed by mixing CDs and GNCs for PNG quantitative determination. As shown in Scheme 1, the fluorescence peaks of the blue-emitting CDs and the red-emissive GNCs are centered at 444 nm and 660 nm with a single excitation at 360 nm, respectively. By mixing CDs and GNCs, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched due to FRET occurring. To confirm the FRET phenomenon, the overlapping of spectra and zeta potentials were investigated. As you can see in Fig. S2, the emission spectrum of CDs was overlapped with the absorption spectrum of GNCs. GNCs and CDs show negative and positive zeta potentials (−32.2 and + 15.8 mV), which attributed to carboxyl and amino groups, respectively. Therefore, the positive charges on the surface of CDs can electrostatically interact with the negative charges on the surface of GNCs.

The effect of PNG was firstly examined on the fluorescence signal of CDs and GNCs, separately. As revealed in Fig. S3, addition of PNG to GNCs and CDs results quenching and no change in fluorescence signals, respectively. After the addition of different concentrations of PNG to mixture of CDs and GNCs, the fluorescence intensity at 660 nm was gradually quenched; while, the fluorescence intensity at 444 nm was unchanged. It can be related to the great affinity of GNCs towards sulfide group of PNG. The possible mechanism for this sensor can be related to the inner filter effect (IFE). Overlapping between the fluorescence excitation spectrum of GNCs and the absorbance spectrum of PNG in Fig. S4 caused to the IFE and resulted in reducing the fluorescence intensity at 660 nm.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism is considered IFE which is corrected using Eq. (1) [37]:
(1)
CF =
F cor
F obs
=
2.3 d
A ex
1 −
10
− d
A ex
10
g
A em
2.3 s
A em
1 −
10
− s
A em
where CF, Fobs and Fcor represent the corrected factor, observed fluorescence intensity and the corrected fluorescence intensity by removing, repetitively. Aex and Aem are the absorbance at λex = 360 nm and λem = 660 nm. The values of d, g and s are set at 1.0, 0.40 and 0.1 cm, respectively. The calculated data were listed in Table S1. According to Fig. 3 , efficiency of IFE for RF system reached to 75 %.

The performance of the RF sensor relied on some experimental variables which must be optimized to improve the sensing efficiency, including (i) the ratio of GNCs to CDs, (ii) pH and (iii) incubation time for PNG. The analytical performance of GNCs/CDs probe was carried out by determining the fluorescence intensity of GNCs and CDs intensity ratio (I660/I444). The amount of GNCs is important and related to the FRET fluorescence quenching process. The changes in the fluorescence quenching of CDs (20 μL, 3 mg/mL) with the introduction of GNCs were demonstrated in Fig. S5. The fluorescence quenching is enhanced by the addition of GNCs. When the amount of GNCs reaches 200 μL (10 mg/mL), the fluorescence quenching efficiency is constant.

In order to find the most sensitive pH for PNG quantitation, the response of the probe in the absence and presence of PNG (50 ppm) was explored over pH 4.5 to 10.0. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the signal intensity of CDs and GNCs were reduced in acidic and alkali media due to the influence of protonation and the conformation changes of ligand BSA induced by pH. In the presence of 20 ppm PNG, a significant quenching was observed for GNCs emission at pH = 7. So, pH at 7 was adjusted as an optimal value.

A rapid recognition is one of crucial aspect of a sensing procedure. So, the effect of incubation time was studied in this detection system. As shown in Fig. 4B, the signal intensity of GNCs was dramatically quenched after the addition of PNG (4 ppm) and remained constant at 11 min. The optimal condition including 200 μL of GNCs to 20 μL of CDs, pH 7 and 11 min of reaction time, led to the maximum quenching of the fluorescence of the sensor.

The performance of the RF sensing system towards PNG was evaluated under optimized experimental conditions. According to Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of GNCs at 660 nm was decreased; in contrast, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 444 nm remained unchanged. A linear response between the I660/I444 value and the PNG concentration was found in two ranges of measurement from 0.1 to 0.8 ppb and 0.8–30.0 ppm (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection (LOD) was obtained to be 0.03 ppm using 3s/slope (s is the standard derivation of blank signals). In addition, the RF probe can also facilitate visual PNG detection. The visual LOD was determined as the first concentration where the color change was distinguishable from the color of the blank. According to images of the RF probe in Fig. 5C and 200 ppb was obtained as visual LOD.

For evaluating the repeatability of the probe, the RF signal of 800 ppb was measured for five repeated measurements. Relative standard deviation (RSD) was attained by 4.6 % The reproducibility of the probe response was explored by preparing three parallel ratiometric probe with the same procedure. The RSD values for 80.0 and 200.0 ppb, 20.0 ppm were calculated 5.59 %, 4.92 %, and 6.47 %, respectively.

The comparison results between the developed RF probe and some previously reported sensors are demonstrated in Table 1 . It can be found that the detection limit is comparable or better than other assays. Furthermore, as a comparison, the advantage of an RF probe is no need for any organic solvents, expensive antibodies and aptamers. In addition, the detection process is fast.

A sensor should not only be sensitive to different concentrations, but also be selective. Selectivity is crucial and necessary for the practical usage of each (bio)sensor. To elucidate the selectivity of the RF probe towards PNG, the influence of several possible interfering species and antibiotics, including Na+, K+, AA, GS, MTZ, TC, Fe3+, Hg2+, V-A, V-E, Lys and CF was explored under same optimized experimental conditions. It can be seen in Fig. 6 A that the PNG (15 ppm) has reduced the ratio of fluorescence intensity I660/I444, whereas other species with the same concentration resulted in no change or reduction in this ratio. Also, Fig. 6B shows these above mentioned substances show slight interference in the detection process. This outcome shows that the RF sensor indicates good selectivity toward PNG.

According to these findings, the ability of the RF sensor to detect PNG in the presence of other possible interfering species was investigated by assessing its performance in the analysis of real samples (milk samples). There was no detectable PNG in any of real samples. Therefore, the standard addition method was implemented. As reported in Table 2 , satisfactory recoveries of PNG calculated by Eq (2) were found 94.4–103 % with RSD below 6.7 % for milk samples. The recovery value more than 100 may be attributed to non-specific response of other molecules in high concentration. These results indicate that the proposed RF sensor can be potentially applied to detect and quantify PNG in the practical samples.
(2)
% Recovery =
Found
amount
Spiked
amount
× 100

Then, the results of PNG detection by the RF probe was analyzed in the intra-day and inter-day. As listed in Table S2, the RSDs are less than 6.8 %, indicating that the proposed method is reliable for monitoring PNG. To investigate the accuracy of the proposed method, the concentrations of PNG in milk samples were measured and compared with HPLC method and reported in Table 3 . The comparison with the standard method for PNG analysis has shown a good correlation.

In summary, the RF probe has been fabricated for rapid analysis of PNG based on a mixture of blue-emissive CDs and red-emissive GNCs in an appropriate ratio. Under optimal condition, PNG can turn off the emission of GNCs, while no change occurred in the emission of CDs. The sensing mechanism is based on IFE. This kind of sensing exhibited the advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity, simple preparation compared with sensors based on single emission. The CDs-GNCs nanoprobe allowed to detect PNG at concentration as low as 0.03 ppm. Additionally, the developed RF technique was also applied to detect PNG in milk samples, revealing great potential application in monitoring of food safety. The RF probe offered a simple and visual assay that can simplify the visual determination of PNG.

Saied Asadpour: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zeinab Saberi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Marzieh Naderi: Visualization, Software, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by the research deputy of Shahrekord university. The grant number was 141/1402/13.
